original_abstract: This paper reports the development of a gold(I)-catalyzed sulfination of aryl and heteroaryl boronic acids. While gold complexes are typically utilized for the activation of $\pi$-systems, this method leverages a redox-neutral catalytic cycle based on the fundamental organometallic reactivity of gold(I)-heteroatom bonds. Key steps in the mechanism include the transmetalation of boronic acids with gold(I) alkoxides—formed in situ from gold(I) halides and base—and the subsequent insertion of sulfur dioxide into the resulting gold(I)-carbon bond. Systematic stoichiometric studies and the isolation of key gold(I)-aryl and gold(I)-sulfinate intermediates support the proposed catalytic pathway. The reaction utilizes potassium metabisulfite as a convenient $SO_2$ source and is compatible with various electronically diverse boronic acids and medicinally relevant heterocycles. Furthermore, the versatility of the generated sulfinate intermediates is demonstrated through their in situ conversion into a library of sulfones and sulfonamides, providing a practical synthetic tool for the construction of important pharmacophores in drug discovery.
humanized_abstract: The gold(I) catalyzed sulfination of aryl and heteroaryl boronic acids is described. Gold catalysis is well established as a highly powerful and selective method for activating $\pi$ systems and has led to the discovery of many new complex molecule constructions. In contrast, here we describe, through a full understanding of mechanism achieved through stoichiometric experiments and the isolation of catalytic gold-aryl and gold-sulfinate species, a gold catalysis reaction based on the common organometallic motif of gold(I) heteroatom bonding, and which operates through a simple redox-neutral transmetalation mechanism involving the reaction of the B(OH) 2 Ar and Au(I)-alkoxide derived from reaction of Au(I) X (X = Cl, Br) with base, SO 2 insertion into the gold(I) metal to carbon bond and a subsequent catalyst turnover by re-transmetalation of the B(OH) 2 Ar species. The gold catalysis reaction was effected with a 1-10 mol % catalytic gold loading using potassium metabisulfite (KHSO 2 O 3 ) as an $SO_2$ source and was general to all boronic acids regardless of electronic character (hyperconjugative, inductive, resonance effects). Heteroaromatic compounds that are highly relevant to medicinal chemistry were also readily sulfinated, allowing access to the corresponding functionalized sulfinate ligands in good to excellent yields that are amenable to further chemical transformation. Importantly, the versatility of the sulfinate species was dramatically demonstrated by a very straightforward and in situ transformation of the sulfinate to sulfones and sulfonamides, precursors to two of the major functional groups that make up a large proportion of the successful drug leads from modern pharmaceutical campaigns.

document.output: The gold(I) catalyzed sulfination of aryl and heteroaryl boronic acids is described. Gold catalysis is well established as a highly powerful and selective method for activating $\pi$ systems and has led to the discovery of many new complex molecule constructions. The gold catalysis reaction was effected with a 1-10 mol % catalytic gold loading using potassium metabisulfite (KHSO 2 O 3 ) as an $SO_2$ source and was general to all boronic acids regardless of electronic character (hyperconjugative, inductive, resonance effects). Heteroaromatic compounds that are highly relevant to medicinal chemistry were also readily sulfinated, allowing access to the corresponding functionalized sulfinate ligands in good to excellent yields that are amenable to further chemical transformation.
